sentences.text: Do you remember I was telling you about Sokoluk, the farm I worked for, where my father took me so I wouldn't be in the ghetto.
It was a horrible winter.
Every winter was horrible-- very cold, very cold.
We had no food.
I decided to go back to Sokoluk on the farm that I worked for nothing.
And I smelled bread, fresh bread with bacon.
And I knocked on the door, the snow was falling and I was cold and hungry.
And I saw her bakin' bread and I ask her if she could give me some bread for the family.
She says, I have no bread for you.
She shut the door in my face.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So when I left, I swore-- I swore I will get even with them somehow.
I didn't know how.
I was a kid, I didn't know.
Another time I remember I walked out from the barn to go-- this was summertime-- for food and I went out through the back of the barn.
[POINTING] It was like a little hill that went down to a creek.
It sloped.
And as I walked down, two Germans were walking behind me with their bayonets.
But they were not after me, they were after chickens.
But when my mother-- my family looked out through the hole, they only saw bayonets coming after me and they thought that was it.
I went, I got my bread, I came back late at night, and my mother and father sittin' shiva.
They sittin' shiva for me because they thought I was a goner.
Then I remember one time in the wintertime, it was bad-- very bad.
My father sent me to this guy named Wedke.
I don't even know how you pronounce it.
He used to work-- he used to drive for my father.
And my father said, go to him.
He's not going to give you out, he'll give you bread.
And I went and it was very cold and I didn't know then that there-- there was a wire between his front door and the barn and there was a dog on that wire.
And the minute I put my hand to open up the door, that dog bit me in my thigh.
I remember looking at his teeth.
His teeth were blue-- not his teeth, his gums were like blue.
And he-- and Wedke opened the door, he saw what was happening.
They were putting snow on it.
I saw every tooth, the dog's tooth in my leg.
I couldn't go to a doctor.
I told Wedke where the families live, and he gave me some bread.
I don't know how I walked back, but I did.
And I came in and my mother and father took one look and my leg was swollen.
And they thought or sure I'm going to die.
But I didn't.
I lived.
It was-- the teeth were there, the marks were there.
My leg was getting bigger and bigger and then it got smaller and smaller and I lived.
I lived.
Nothing.
We had nothing to wrap it.
We had nothing-- nothing.
I was the oldest.
My sister was only 11 and she-- when I look back, she always-- every day, she said, why don't we give ourselves up?
Why don't we get ourselves up?
I mean, look at us, she said, we're-- and I would smack her face many a times.
I would say, don't talk like this.
Do not even talk like this.

The boys could not go out because if they caught-- that they were more known than a girl and if they would catch a boy, they were circumcised so they knew right away that they-- you know, they were Jewish.
So they send me.
I was the older.
One day-- believe it or not, this was a month before we were liberated, but we didn't know we were going to be liberated-- Unubra's son, he had two sons and a daughter.
The daughter was very nice.
One son was in Germany in a labor camp working as a [INAUDIBLE] and one was a-- he was no good.
He was a collaborator with the Germans.
And he told Unubra at the farm, he says, you get rid of the Jews because I'll give them out if you don't get rid of them.
So they had to leave.
Unubra comes in cryin'.
He was such a good man.
He was cryin'.
And he said, you'll have to leave because my son, of all people, is going to give you out.
So I went out.
One day I went out for bread and I saw this farm on a hill all by itself.
His name was Yaski, the guy that owned it.
Can you spell that?
Y-A-S-K-E.
He was Polish.
He lived with his wife on that farm.
But at night he would go into Mlynow because he was afraid of the Ukrainians-- they're going to come and burn his farm down because there was fights going on between Ukrainians and Polish.
Mlynow was my hometown and there were more Polacks there so he was safer there than the farm.
Because if they would burn down the farm, he wouldn't-- he would die.
They would kill him.
So I came back and I told my father.
I says, I found a place for us where to go, Yaski.
And my father says, oh, we have no choice.
Tonight we're leaving here.
And it was wintertime It was cold, snow.
[SIGHS]
Nothing.
We had nothing.
We had our-- me and my sister two little linen dresses, galoshes-- nothing.
And we walked in the snow through the farm.
We didn't walk on the road, we walked through like a farm.
We heard some kind of shooting at night, we didn't know where it-- it came from.
And we finally made it to that farm, to Yaski's farm.
And we crawled in under the straw.
We were hungry and we were dirty and we were cold.
We all crawled into the straw and we stayed there.
And we were worried about footstep--steps but God was with us again.
The snow started to fall and covered all the footstep.
In the morning, we heard Yaski and his-- well, not his wife, just him.
He came and took the barn and my father came out from hiding.
And he said, Yaski, I'm here with my family.
You have to save me.
And he went in the kitchen and he told his wife and she cooked a real big pot of potatoes.
And he brought it out and [LAUGHS] we devoured those potatoes like that.
And she came out.
She was happy to see my father, my mother.
My mother and father were very-- it was a small town.
And we knew the war-- and so we talked about we couldn't stay on the farm because of the Ukrain-- they come at night to burn the farm, we'll die.
So they came up with an idea that they're going to-- they had a-- where they used to keep potatoes.
It was like a cellar in the-- in the ground.

They said if they would move all these potatoes from one side to the other and put some boards up and then put the potatoes back on the boards, so even if they burned the farm, we would not die because we were underground.
Well, when we went there.

We-- the boys, my two brothers, and my father, Yaski himself, they moved all these potatoes to one side and they put boards and they put the potatoes and they made a little closing door like you could crawl in and then you-- they-- it's like a little drawer with potatoes and they just put it on top.
So if somebody comes in and looks at potatoes, it's potatoes all over.
But the floors were cement and we laid in the cold on the cement.
I remember my mother-- my mother was-- she didn't-- she was cold and-- but this is how we slept on nights.
During the day, we would go out to the barn, but at night we laid there under those potatoes and we heard the bombings.
We already heard the shots and we knew that the war was coming to an end.
And the closer the bombings came, the happier we got.
And one day, we were in the barn on top, like under the-- under the roof like a crawl space and we saw these soldiers riding up on horses with bayonets.
We didn't know what in the world was going on because we never thought that we would ever be free again.
They were Russian soldiers.
The front was-- was right there.
And we looked up and we saw these Germans on the road, but that was afterwards.
Anyway, the-- the Russians came that farm.
They-- and they made their headquarters in that farm.
And this handsome man, tall handsome man, says to my father, who are you?
And my father says, I'm a Jew.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And he says, you're a Jew?
He says, I traveled 10,000 kilometers and I haven't seen a Jew.
He says, oh, my.
He says-- he was very nice to us.
What was his name?
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
And we stayed with him.
He said, do you know anybody that has a good farm here with a lot of horses and cows because we need it for our soldiers?
And that's when Sokoluk came into my mind-- the one that wouldn't give me no bread.
That's when I got even.
I said, I have the perfect place.
And I drew a little map for the Russian soldiers and they went up and they cleaned them out of every horse, cow, and pig.
That was my way.
And a couple of days later, he said to my father, we are strong, the Russians, and we don't expect to be pulling back.
But you never know.
He says, you lived through so much.
He says, you have to go about 50 kilometers in behind the lines.
So he gave us a Russian soldier, a driver, and a wagon and they took us.
And then the wagon couldn't get through because the German tanks were running back.
And we saw-- I actually saw-- we were walking on bodies because the bodies were frozen.
We couldn't walk.
The-- the Russian tanks were going towards the front and the Germans were running back.
It was hell.
And we were actually walking all over bodies.
Wintertime, yeah.
Wintertime.
I think it was-- I think it was-- I'm not sure.
I think it was March of '44.
And we got to Rowne.
That was one of the bigger towns near us that-- and I don't know how, but we found a few other Jews, survivors.
Rowne was a big, beautiful, very Jewish city.
They were all gone.
There were just a few.
And my mother found her brother.
My mother walked everywhere, went in one of the houses, and she her brother.
He had lost a wife and six children, but he survived.
And that was it, the war was over.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't go back to my hometown because they bombed my house.
My house which was good was flat, so we went to Dubno, where my grandmother and my cousins and my aunts lived.
And naturally, nobody was there-- nobody.
Because-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --we just knew.
Somebody I guess must have told us.
But then again, when we lived in Dubno, I wanted to go see for myself.
So I went on a truck, on a Russian truck that went to Mlynow.
I hitchhiked and went to Mlynow.

And I never told this to even my parents, but I went to the cemetery and I wanted to see if my brother's, what do you call them, [NON-ENGLISH] was there, you know his marker was there because they knocked-- the Germans knocked down so many of them.
And it was there because it was a slab.
It was a flat slab.
And my-- I never told my parents this.
And from the cemetery, I went into town and actually, the house was gone and it was horrible-- was horrible.
And I went right back.
I went right back to Dubno.
And we didn't stay long in Dubno.
From Dubno we went to Biton, Poland.
That was like on the border with Germany.
And...
Yeah.
And from there we went to Furnwald, Germany.
We stayed two years in a displaced person camp.
And from Furnwald, we came here.
I believe it's F-U-R-N-W-A-L-D, Furnwald.
It's near Munich.
Near Munich.
We were together.
Yeah.
We were together.
Well, we were free.
It was-- it was-- compared with what we went through, it was-- was wonderful.
There were Jews from all over the world.
Every street there had different Jews.
Our street was all from Poland, another street was from Hungary, another street was from Romania.
And we made the best.
We made the best.
It was not easy.
It was not an easy life, but we made the-- we were free.
We were liberated.
That was, you know, the best part.
And-- and then we came here.
Oh.
It was-- it was not a pleasant journey.
We went on the which broke down halfway.
And the first day we were 900 Jews on the ship.
The next day, I think there were three of them because everybody was sick.
Everybody was sick.
Everybody was laying-- it broke down-- the ship broke down, we had to go back to England.
They gave us another ship, the and we came over.
It took us a month, a month.
And we came here in April 1st, 1947.
In-- well, we-- came in to New York.
But my uncle-- I had an uncle here, Jack Kozak.
He came and picked us up and brought us to Philadelphia.
My whole family, yeah.
[SIGHS] When did I meet my husband?
I-- I went to a dance one night with a friend of mine.
They had dances at the [?Broadwood?].
Every Sunday night they had dances-- you know, Jewish crowd, you know.
And I went there with a friend of mine and they-- he went with a couple of boyfriends to a wedding and they left the wedding early and they came and they were-- then the rest is history.
I got married August the 13th of 1950.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I worked.
I knew the streets were not paved with gold.
In those days, nobody gave you anything.
So I worked.
I worked in a shop.
A man's shop where they made men's clothing.
And the boss said to me, you know how to sew?
I said, of course.
I didn't know.
But I started, I worked piecework.
I worked very well, very good.
And then I went to the mountains.
They-- I went-- I didn't want to stay in Philadelphia and so I went to the mountains and I met a friend of mine, Betty [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know whatever happened to her.
We lost contact.
And she says, you know what?
We ought to go work in the Catskills.
You get tips.
I said, what is tips?
I was a real greenhorn.
And she says, you know, if you're good, you get-- they give you money.
So I went to the Catskills and I worked there.
And I made good money, good money.
I worked very, very hard.
But you know, it's hard when you don't speak English.
You don't know the language, you don't know the ways of the country.
But you learn.
You learn.
Anything that I could do.
I was a bus girl because I couldn't-- I couldn't be a waitress because I couldn't speak English.
I was a bus girl, I was-- you name it, I did it.
And I worked very hard, very hard.
And at the end of the day-- the end of the week, Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE] would leave me $10.
And all of the college boys I worked there with say, how did you manage to get $10?
And I said, well, when her tea was hot, she wanted it cold I ran for an ice cube.
You don't do it, [LAUGHS] you know.
And that's-- you know, I didn't speak English, so that helped.
About 18 and a half.
About 18 and a half.
I had no big wedding.
I-- I think the rabbi was Rabbi Rosen.
He passed away soon.
I got married in a polka dot dress, a navy blue polka dot dress, you know.
And my mother-in-law, she's still living, thank god, she made-- she made a little party in her house and this was our wedding, you know.
And-- I have a wonderful daughter today, Miriam; a wonderful granddaughter, Lisa.
Marvin, but everybody calls him Sonny.
Everybody calls him Sonny.
Well, he had gas stations and then he had taxi cabs.
He owned a couple of taxi cabs.
And he sold them.
He didn't really-- it was-- it was getting too dangerous.
And now he's just-- he's retired, more or less.
But he drives a limo once in a while, you know.
Gives him something to do.
He's too young to retire.
Yeah.
Well, when-- when I first moved to this house for-- we joined Shaare Shamayim, the synagogue around the corner.
I was [? the only chairman of ?] sisterhood of Shaare Shamayim.
It was nice.
You know, when you're young.
But you know, but then when you get older, you know.
Now I don't belong to the synagogue.
And--
No.
No.
No.
No.
I never kept kosher.
I buy from the kosher butcher.
You know, I go to the kosher butcher to buy, but I never kept kosher.
I don't know.
I sometimes think of all the kids, they were-- I wasn't better than them.
They were beautiful children.
Even the ones that were ugly became beautiful.
My mother used to say, look at them.
[SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] They all got so pretty.
And so I think of them sometimes, why did I survive and they didn't?
I don't know.
I have no answer for that.
I have no answer for that.
They were good people, very good, very good God-fearing people.
They were good people.
And I'm glad to be here today to tell the story.
For those that say it never happened, it happened.
I was there, it happened.
I hope it never happens again.
I don't want my children or my children's children ever to go through anything what we went through.
What I told you are just a few stories that I can remember more than.
But there were so many, there was so many others.
Like one day I went to this farmer that my-- he was a Czech, very nice man, very nice.
He also saved a lot of Jews.
And I saw a girlfriend, a neighbor that grew up across the street from me with a big cross around her neck.
And I just looked at her and she looked at me and we never said a word.
We couldn't say a word to each other.
And she lives in Israel today.
She says, I'm going to church.
I said, so am I.
You always had to be one step-- I don't know.
It's not that you were smart.
You always had to be one step.
Maybe it's the way life was, which always-- I went to church.
I went to church with her.
I did everything that what they did and I walked there.
There are so many stories, so many.
So many.
But we are the lucky ones.
We are the lucky ones.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I can't think.
[SIGHS] I can't think of any right now.
I can't think.
As I'm saying-- I'm saying all these things, I'm saying, did I really do all, did I live through all this?
It's so unbelievable, unbelievable.
Unbelievable.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
My pleasure.
My pleasure.
The story has to be told.
[SIGHS] I hope no Jewish child ever goes through anything what we went through there.
